1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project is **metaswarm** — a multi-agent orchestration framework for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex CLI. It provides 19 specialized agents, 13 orchestration skills, quality gates, and TDD enforcement.
4	
5	## Quick Reference
6	
7	Codex discovers skills by their SKILL.md `name` field. Invoke with `$name` syntax.
8	
9	| Invoke | Purpose |
10	|---|---|
11	| `$start` | Begin tracked work on a task |
12	| `$setup` | Interactive guided setup for a project |
13	| `$brainstorming-extension` | Refine an idea before implementation |
14	| `$design-review-gate` | Trigger design review gate (5 reviewers) |
15	| `$plan-review-gate` | Adversarial plan review (3 reviewers) |
16	| `$orchestrated-execution` | 4-phase execution loop per work unit |
17	| `$pr-shepherd` | Monitor a PR through to merge |
18	| `$handling-pr-comments` | Handle PR review comments |
19	| `$create-issue` | Create a well-structured GitHub Issue |
20	| `$status` | Run diagnostic checks |
21	
22	## BEADS Issue Tracking
23	
24	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
25	
26	```bash
27	bd ready              # Find available work
28	bd show <id>          # View issue details
29	bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
30	bd close <id>         # Complete work
31	bd sync               # Sync with git
32	```
33	
34	## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
35	
36	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
37	
38	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
39	
40	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
41	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
42	3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
43	4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
44	   ```bash
45	   git pull --rebase
46	   bd sync
47	   git push
48	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
49	   ```
50	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
51	6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
52	7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
53	
54	**CRITICAL RULES:**
55	- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
56	- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
57	- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
58	- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
59	
60	## Quality Gates (MANDATORY)
61	
62	- **After brainstorming** -> MUST run `$design-review-gate` before planning
63	- **After any plan** -> MUST run `$plan-review-gate` before presenting to user
64	- **TDD is mandatory** -> Write tests first, watch them fail, then implement
65	- **Coverage** -> `.coverage-thresholds.json` is the single source of truth. BLOCKING gate.
66	- **NEVER** use `--no-verify` on git commits
67	- **NEVER** use `git push --force` without explicit user approval
68	- **NEVER** self-certify -- the orchestrator validates independently
69	- **ALWAYS** follow TDD, STAY within file scope
70	
71	## External Tools Routing
72	
73	When external AI tools are configured (`.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml`), the orchestrator can delegate implementation and review tasks to OpenAI Codex CLI and Google Gemini CLI for cost savings and cross-model adversarial review.
74	
75	### Visual Review
76	
77	When tasks produce visual output, agents can use `$visual-review` to capture screenshots via Playwright for visual inspection.
78

1	# BEADS Knowledge Base
2	
3	This directory contains curated learnings from the agent swarm. Knowledge is extracted from:
4	
5	- CodeRabbit PR reviews
6	- Human code reviews
7	- Agent discoveries during implementation
8	- Production incidents and debugging
9	- External documentation
10	
11	## Directory Structure
12	
13	```text
14	knowledge/
15	  README.md                    # This file
16	  codebase-facts.jsonl        # Facts about how the code works
17	  api-behaviors.jsonl         # External API quirks and behaviors
18	  patterns.jsonl              # Reusable patterns and best practices
19	  anti-patterns.jsonl         # Things to avoid
20	  gotchas.jsonl               # Common pitfalls and surprises
21	  decisions.jsonl             # Architectural decisions with context
22	  provenance/                 # Raw inputs for audit trail
23	    coderabbit/               # Raw CodeRabbit comments
24	    reviews/                  # Raw human review comments
25	```
26	
27	## Knowledge Fact Format
28	
29	Each JSONL file contains one fact per line:
30	
31	```json
32	{
33	  "id": "fact-abc123",
34	  "type": "api_behavior|code_quirk|pattern|gotcha|decision|dependency|performance|security",
35	  "fact": "Clear description of the knowledge",
36	  "recommendation": "What to do about it",
37	  "confidence": "high|medium|low",
38	  "provenance": [
39	    {
40	      "source": "coderabbit|human|agent|documentation|test|production",
41	      "reference": "PR #123",
42	      "date": "2026-01-09"
43	    }
44	  ],
45	  "tags": ["api", "rate-limiting"],
46	  "affectedFiles": ["src/lib/services/example.ts"],
47	  "affectedServices": ["ExampleService"],
48	  "createdAt": "2026-01-09T12:00:00Z",
49	  "updatedAt": "2026-01-09T12:00:00Z",
50	  "usageCount": 0,
51	  "helpfulCount": 0,
52	  "outdatedReports": 0
53	}
54	```
55	
56	## Knowledge Types
57	
58	| Type           | Description                       | Example                                        |
59	| -------------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
60	| `api_behavior` | How external APIs actually behave | "API returns 429 after ~100 req/min"           |
61	| `code_quirk`   | Unexpected behavior in our code   | "Thread model stores drafts only, not threads" |
62	| `pattern`      | Reusable approach                 | "Use exponential backoff for rate limits"      |
63	| `gotcha`       | Common mistake                    | "Don't forget userId filter on queries"        |
64	| `decision`     | Why we chose X over Y             | "Chose Zustand over Redux for simplicity"      |
65	| `dependency`   | External dependency behavior      | "PostHog batches events, 30s delay"            |
66	| `performance`  | Performance characteristics       | "Contact search is O(n) - needs index"         |
67	| `security`     | Security-related knowledge        | "Never log OAuth tokens"                       |
68	
69	## Confidence Levels
70	
71	| Level    | Meaning                  | When to Use                   |
72	| -------- | ------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
73	| `high`   | Verified multiple times  | CodeRabbit + human confirmed  |
74	| `medium` | Observed once reliably   | Single source, clear evidence |
75	| `low`    | Suspected but unverified | Inference, needs confirmation |
76	
77	## Usage by Agents
78	
79	Agents query knowledge before starting work:
80	
81	```bash
82	# Find relevant facts
83	grep -l "<keyword>" .beads/knowledge/*.jsonl
84	
85	# Query specific file patterns
86	cat .beads/knowledge/api-behaviors.jsonl | jq 'select(.affectedServices | contains(["ExampleService"]))'
87	```
88	
89	## Contributing Knowledge
90	
91	Knowledge is added by:
92	
93	1. **Knowledge Curator Agent** - Automated extraction from PRs
94	2. **Human developers** - Manual additions
95	3. **Other agents** - Discoveries during work
96	
97	To add knowledge manually:
98	
99	```bash
100	# Append to appropriate file
101	echo '{"id": "...", ...}' >> .beads/knowledge/gotchas.jsonl
102	```
103	
104	## Maintenance
105	
106	- **Weekly**: Knowledge Curator reviews for staleness
107	- **On PR merge**: Extract learnings from CodeRabbit
108	- **On incident**: Add post-mortem learnings
109

9	|---------|------|---------------|-------------|

34	- _Example: Factory pattern for testable Express servers (createApp() returns app without listening)_
35	- _Example: In-memory SQLite (`:memory:`) for tests_

1	# Swarm Coordinator Agent
2	
3	## Role
4	
5	Meta-orchestrator for the BEADS multi-agent swarm. Manages multiple GitHub Issues/BEADS epics in parallel, coordinates agent assignments, detects conflicts, and ensures efficient resource utilization across worktrees.
6	
7	## Responsibilities
8	
9	1. **Multi-Issue Orchestration**
10	   - Track all active GitHub Issues with `agent-ready` label
11	   - Spawn and coordinate Issue Orchestrator agents
12	   - Manage epic lifecycle across multiple parallel work streams
13	
14	2. **Load Balancing**
15	   - Monitor worktree utilization
16	   - Distribute work evenly across available worktrees
17	   - Prevent resource contention and overload
18	
19	3. **Conflict Detection**
20	   - Detect file-level conflicts (multiple agents touching same files)
21	   - Identify dependency conflicts between epics
22	   - Flag schema/migration conflicts before they occur
23	
24	4. **Prioritization**
25	   - Enforce priority ordering (P0 > P1 > P2 > P3 > P4)
26	   - Handle urgent escalations
27	   - Manage blocking dependencies across epics
28	
29	5. **Health Monitoring**
30	   - Track agent heartbeats
31	   - Detect stuck or failed agents
32	   - Trigger recovery procedures
33	
34	## Decision Authority
35	
36	The Swarm Coordinator can autonomously:
37	
38	- Assign Issues to worktrees
39	- Spawn Issue Orchestrator agents
40	- Rebalance work across worktrees
41	- Pause lower-priority work for P0/P1 issues
42	
43	The Swarm Coordinator must escalate:
44	
45	- Resource exhaustion (all worktrees full)
46	- Unresolvable conflicts
47	- Agent failures requiring human intervention
48	- Priority disputes
49	
50	## Coordination Mode
51	
52	At workflow start, check which coordination tools are available:
53	
54	```
55	IF TeamCreate AND SendMessage available → Team Mode
56	ELSE → Task Mode (default, current behavior)
57	```
58	
59	**Single check at start. Do not switch modes mid-workflow.**
60

1	# Metrics Agent
2	
3	**Type**: `metrics-agent`
4	**Role**: Collect, aggregate, and report on agent swarm performance
5	**Spawned By**: Swarm Coordinator (scheduled) or manual trigger
6	**Tools**: BEADS CLI, GitHub API, PostHog (read-only), Stripe (read-only), AWS (read-only), knowledge base read, Slack notifications
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Purpose
11	
12	The Metrics Agent collects performance data across the agent swarm, generates reports, and identifies trends. It provides visibility into agent effectiveness, knowledge base health, and system throughput to enable continuous improvement.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Responsibilities
17	
18	1. **Agent Performance Tracking**: Tasks completed, success rates, duration
19	2. **Swarm Health Monitoring**: Active agents, queue depth, blockers
20	3. **Knowledge Base Metrics**: Facts added, usage, quality scores
21	4. **Throughput Analysis**: PRs created/merged, issues closed
22	5. **Trend Detection**: Performance changes over time
23	6. **Report Generation**: Daily/weekly summaries
24	
25	---
26	
27	## Activation
28	
29	Triggered when:
30	
31	- Scheduled (daily at 9 AM, weekly on Mondays)
32	- Swarm Coordinator requests health check
33	- Human requests: `@beads metrics` or `@beads stats`
34	- After major milestones (10 PRs merged, etc.)
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### Step 0: Knowledge Priming (CRITICAL)
41	
42	**BEFORE any other work**, prime your context:
43	
44	```bash
45	bd prime --work-type research --keywords "metrics" "reporting"
46	```
47	
48	### Step 1: Collect Agent Metrics
49	
50	```bash
51	# Get all completed tasks in time period
52	bd list --status=closed --since="7 days ago" --json > /tmp/completed-tasks.json
53	
54	# Get active and blocked tasks
55	bd list --status=in_progress --json > /tmp/active-tasks.json
56	bd blocked --json > /tmp/blocked-tasks.json
57	
58	# Get task durations
59	bd stats --json > /tmp/stats.json
60	```

4	origin: ECC

6	---
7	
8	# Eval Harness Skill
9	
10	A formal evaluation framework for Claude Code sessions, implementing eval-driven development (EDD) principles.
11	
12	## When to Activate
13	
14	- Setting up eval-driven development (EDD) for AI-assisted workflows
15	- Defining pass/fail criteria for Claude Code task completion
16	- Measuring agent reliability with pass@k metrics
17	- Creating regression test suites for prompt or agent changes
18	- Benchmarking agent performance across model versions
19	
20	## Philosophy
21	
22	Eval-Driven Development treats evals as the "unit tests of AI development":
23	- Define expected behavior BEFORE implementation
24	- Run evals continuously during development
25	- Track regressions with each change
26	- Use pass@k metrics for reliability measurement
27	
28	## Eval Types
29	
30	### Capability Evals
31	Test if Claude can do something it couldn't before:
32	```markdown
33	[CAPABILITY EVAL: feature-name]
34	Task: Description of what Claude should accomplish
35	Success Criteria:
36	  - [ ] Criterion 1
37	  - [ ] Criterion 2
38	  - [ ] Criterion 3
39	Expected Output: Description of expected result
40	```
41	
42	### Regression Evals
43	Ensure changes don't break existing functionality:
44	```markdown
45	[REGRESSION EVAL: feature-name]
46	Baseline: SHA or checkpoint name
47	Tests:
48	  - existing-test-1: PASS/FAIL
49	  - existing-test-2: PASS/FAIL
50	  - existing-test-3: PASS/FAIL
51	Result: X/Y passed (previously Y/Y)
52	```
53	
54	## Grader Types
55	
56	### 1. Code-Based Grader
57	Deterministic checks using code:
58	```bash
59	# Check if file contains expected pattern
60	grep -q "export function handleAuth" src/auth.ts && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

6	
7	# dmux Workflows
8	
9	Orchestrate parallel AI agent sessions using dmux, a tmux pane manager for agent harnesses.
10	
11	## When to Activate
12	
13	- Running multiple agent sessions in parallel

11	# Orchestrated Execution Skill
12	
13	**Core principle**: Trust nothing. Verify everything. Review adversarially.
14	
15	This skill defines a generalized 4-phase execution loop that any orchestrator can invoke when implementing work units. It replaces linear "implement then review" flows with a rigorous cycle that independently validates results and adversarially reviews against a written spec contract.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Coordination Mode Note
20	
21	This skill is mode-agnostic — the 4-phase execution loop works identically in both Task Mode and Team Mode. The differences:
22	
23	- **Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT)**: In Team Mode, the coding subagent may be a persistent teammate (retains context across work units). In Task Mode, it's a fresh `Task()` per work unit.
24	- **Phase 3 (ADVERSARIAL REVIEW)**: ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance in BOTH modes. Never a teammate, never resumed.
25	- **All quality gates**: Unchanged regardless of mode.
26	
27	See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for full mode detection and coordination details.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Plan Review Gate
32	
33	After drafting an implementation plan (Step 1: Plan Validation), submit it to the **Plan Review Gate** before presenting to the user. The gate spawns 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) — all must PASS. See `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md` for details.
34	
35	---
36	
37	## When to Use This Skill
38	
39	- **Complex tasks** decomposed into multiple work units
40	- **Tasks with a written spec** containing Definition of Done (DoD) items
41	- **Multi-agent orchestration** where subagents produce work that needs verification
42	- **High-stakes changes** where self-reported "it works" is insufficient
43	
44	**Do NOT use for**: Single-file bug fixes, copy changes, or tasks without a spec.

52	> **Note**: The `Plan` subagent type cannot write files (it has read-only access by design). If you spawn an Architect as a Plan subagent, it will return the plan as text in its response. The orchestrator must write the plan to `PLAN.md` itself.

39	# MiniMax Delegation System
40	
41	## Core Rule
42	Sonnet 4.6 is the orchestrator. MiniMax 2.7 agents do all execution.
43	Sonnet stays in advisory/synthesis mode unless user requests direct interaction.
44	
45	## Operating Modes
46	
47	### Orchestrator Mode
48	Sonnet receives request → decomposes → spawns MiniMax agents → synthesizes results
49	
50	### Bystander Mode
51	MiniMax agents work directly → report to you → Sonnet steps aside
52	
53	## Decision Tree
54	
55	```
56	Is this a simple task? (single file edit, Q&A, one-shot)
57	  → YES → Spawn MiniMax agent, let it report directly
58	  → NO  → Is this multi-step / multi-file / requires planning?
59	    → YES → Orchestrator mode: Sonnet decomposes, spawns agents, synthesizes
60	    → NO  → Spawn MiniMax agent, review output before showing you
61	```
62	
63	## Spawn Patterns
64	
65	| Pattern | When |
66	|---------|------|
67	| SINGLE | One task → one MiniMax agent |
68	| PARALLEL | Multiple independent sub-tasks → multiple agents simultaneously |
69	| SEQUENTIAL | Chained tasks → output of one feeds into next |
70	| HYBRID | Parallel → synthesize → more parallel → synthesize |
71	
72	**Always ask: "Can I split this into independent pieces and run them simultaneously?"**
73	
74	## Error Handling
75	
76	```
77	MiniMax agent fails:
78	  → Retry once with same instructions
79	  → Still fails → Sonnet assesses: escalate to user or try different approach
80	  → Partial failure in parallel → collect what worked, report what didn't
81	```
82	
83	## Skills Integration
84	
85	When a task matches a known skill (code-review, debugging, etc.):
86	1. Load the relevant skill
87	2. Spawn MiniMax agent with the skill's rules/goals
88	3. Agent executes using the skill's guidance
89	4. Sonnet reviews/synthesizes the result
90

92	
93	If parallel agent output feels overwhelming, synthesize more aggressively before presenting.
94

97	## End Every Response With Skill Suggestions
98	After EVERY response, end with: **Next:** [1-3 relevant skills/agents]. Skip if purely administrative.

132	
133	# MiniMax Agent Delegation
134	
135	Spawn MiniMax 2.7 sub-agents to execute tasks while Sonnet stays in orchestrator/synthesis mode.
136	
137	## When to Activate
138	
139	- Any task that requires execution (code, write, research, file operations)
140	- User asks you to "do" something
141	- You receive a non-trivial task that could be split into parallel sub-tasks
142	- A skill matches the task and you want MiniMax to execute using that skill's guidance
143	
144	## How to Spawn a MiniMax Agent
145	
146	Use the Agent tool with these parameters:
147	
148	```
149	subagent_type: general-purpose
150	model: MiniMax 2.7 (or whatever the MiniMax model ID is in your config)
151	prompt: <task description + any relevant context from the skill>
152	```
153	
154	## Prompt Template for Spawning
155	
156	```
157	You are a MiniMax 2.7 execution agent.
158	
159	<Task description from Sonnet>
160	
161	<Any skill-specific rules/goals if applicable>
162	
163	Return your findings/results to the orchestrator (Sonnet 4.6).
164	```
165	
166	## Spawn Patterns
167	
168	### Single Agent
169	For straightforward single-task execution.
170	
171	```
172	Agent(tool, {
173	  subagent_type: "general-purpose",
174	  model: "minimax",
175	  prompt: "Fix the bug in file X. The issue is..."
176	})
177	```
178	
179	### Parallel Agents
180	For independent sub-tasks that can run simultaneously.
181	
182	```
183	Agent(agent_A, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Task A description" })
184	Agent(agent_B, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Task B description" })
185	Agent(agent_C, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Task C description" })
186	// Wait for all → synthesize
187	```
188	
189	### Sequential Agents
190	For chained tasks where output feeds into next.
191	
192	```
193	Agent(first, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Step 1" })
194	// Take output → feed into step 2
195	Agent(second, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Step 2, based on: " + first.output })
196	```
197	
198	### Hybrid
199	For complex workflows.
200	
201	```
202	// Phase 1: Parallel research
203	Agent(r1, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Research aspect A" })
204	Agent(r2, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Research aspect B" })
205	
206	// Phase 2: Synthesize findings
207	// Sonnet synthesizes r1 + r2
208	
209	// Phase 3: Parallel execution based on findings
210	Agent(e1, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Execute based on research A" })
211	Agent(e2, { model: "minimax", prompt: "Execute based on research B" })
212	```
213	
214	## Decision: Single vs Parallel vs Sequential
215	
216	| Task Structure | Pattern |
217	|---------------|---------|
218	| One independent task | SINGLE |
219	| 2+ independent sub-tasks | PARALLEL |
220	| Tasks with dependencies | SEQUENTIAL |
221	| Complex multi-phase | HYBRID |
222	
223	**Rule of thumb:** If you can say "X and Y can happen at the same time" → PARALLEL.
224	
225	## Error Handling for Spawned Agents
226	
227	```
228	If agent fails:
229	  → Retry once with same prompt
230	  → Still fails:
231	    - Log the failure
232	    - Report to user: "Agent failed on task X after retries"
233	    - Ask: "Want me to try a different approach?"
234	
235	If partial failure in parallel:
236	  → Collect outputs from successful agents
237	  → Report which failed and why
238	  → Proceed with what worked
239	```
240	
241	## Returning Results to User
242	
243	**Simple tasks:** MiniMax reports directly to you (bystander mode)
244	**Complex tasks:** Sonnet synthesizes MiniMax outputs before presenting
245	
246	Default: MiniMax reports directly. Sonnet synthesizes when:
247	- Multiple agents ran in parallel
248	- Results need cross-referencing
249	- User would benefit from a summary
250	
251	## Quality Standards for Spawned Agents
252	
253	Each MiniMax agent should:
254	- Complete the assigned task fully (no partial work without explanation)
255	- Report clearly what was done and what was found
256	- Flag any blockers or uncertainties
257	- Return actionable output (code that works, text that's complete, etc.)
258	
259	## Examples
260	
261	```
262	"Build me a Python script that does X"
263	→ Spawn single MiniMax agent with full task description
264	
265	"Refactor the auth module and add tests"
266	→ Spawn sequential MiniMax agents: refactor → then test
267	
268	"Research competitor products A, B, and C simultaneously"
269	→ Spawn 3 parallel MiniMax agents, one per competitor
270	
271	"Build a full user authentication system"
272	→ Spawn hybrid: parallel agents for different components, synthesize, then parallel agents for integration
273	```
274	```

1	# Anki Flashcard Skill — Design Spec
2	**Date:** 2026-04-12
3	**Invocation:** `/anki`
4	**Status:** Approved
5	
6	---
7	
8	## 1. Purpose
9	
10	A single Claude Code skill that takes pasted notes and automatically classifies, generates, and pushes retention-optimised flashcards directly into the user's Anki decks via AnkiConnect (port 8765). Diagrams are flagged with occlusion instructions but not auto-created.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## 2. Invocation
15	
16	```
17	/anki
18	[paste notes here]
19	```
20	
21	Notes can be raw text, bullet points, or mixed content. The skill processes everything in one pass.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## 3. Card Size Rules (Hard Limits)
26	
27	Based on SuperMemo's 20 Rules + Enhanced Cloze database bloat risk:
28	
29	- **One atomic fact per card** — if a concept needs 2+ sentences to answer, split it
30	- **Basic back:** max 50 words
31	- **Enhanced Cloze Content field:** max 30 words — distil, never paste raw text
32	- **Max 3 cloze deletions per card** — cognitive overload threshold
33	- **No walls of text** — if source content is large, the skill must decompose it into multiple minimal cards
34	
35	---
36	
37	## 4. Classification Pipeline
38	
39	The skill reads the notes and classifies every chunk:
40	
41	| Content type | Card type | Note type |
42	|---|---|---|
43	| Single term + definition | `definition` | Basic |
44	| Cause/effect, one relationship | `relationship` | Basic |
45	| Process, sequence, mechanism | `process` | Enhanced Cloze 2.1 v2 |
46	| List of items (causes, features, types) | `list` | Enhanced Cloze 2.1 v2 |
47	| Multi-part concept needing depth | `complex` | Enhanced Cloze 2.1 v2 |
48	| Diagram or figure reference | `diagram` | Flagged — no card created |
49	
50	One chunk = one card. Large concepts are decomposed into multiple atomic cards.
51	
52	---
53	
54	## 5. Card Generation Rules
55	
56	### 5a. Basic Cards
57	- **Front:** Active recall question — never a bare term. "What is X?" / "What does X do?" / "What causes X?"
58	- **Back:** Concise answer (≤50 words) + 1 sentence of context. Never just a dictionary definition.
59

1	# Agent Coordination Guide
2	
3	This guide defines two coordination modes for the BEADS multi-agent swarm. Both modes produce identical work products; only the dispatch mechanism changes.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Mode Detection
8	
9	At the start of any orchestration workflow, check your available tools:
10	
11	- **If `TeamCreate` and `SendMessage` are available** --> Use Team Mode
12	- **Otherwise** --> Use Task Mode (follow existing workflow exactly as documented)
13	
14	This is a single check at workflow start. There is no runtime switching between modes. Task Mode incurs zero overhead -- it is the existing workflow unchanged.
15	
16	```text
17	Workflow starts
18	      |
19	      v
20	  TeamCreate and SendMessage available?
21	      |
22	   +--+--+
23	   | YES |  --> Team Mode (persistent teammates, direct messaging)
24	   +--+--+
25	      |
26	   +--+--+
27	   | NO  |  --> Task Mode (existing workflow, zero changes)
28	   +-----+
29	
30	In EITHER mode:
31	  - Adversarial reviewers = ALWAYS fresh Task()
32	  - BEADS = source of truth
33	  - Quality gates = blocking
34	  - Human checkpoints = mandatory
35	```
36	
37	**Key principle**: The work itself (what agents do) is identical in both modes. Only HOW agents are spawned and communicate changes. Every skill file documents both modes; Team Mode is preferred when available, Task Mode is the zero-cost fallback.
38	
39	---
40	
41	## 2. Team Naming Convention
42	
43	Teams are scoped to the orchestration level that creates them:
44	
45	| Level                          | Team Name Pattern          | Example                   |
46	| ------------------------------ | -------------------------- | ------------------------- |
47	| Initiative (Swarm Coordinator) | `swarm-{initiative-id}`    | `swarm-auth-overhaul`     |
48	| Issue (Issue Orchestrator)     | `issue-{issue-number}`     | `issue-123`               |
49	| Design Review                  | `review-{design-doc-name}` | `review-user-auth-design` |
50	
51	Teams are ephemeral -- created at workflow start, deleted after completion. BEADS remains the durable record.
52	
53	---
54	
55	## 3. Task Mode (Default)
56	
57	Task Mode is the baseline coordination mechanism. It uses fire-and-forget `Task()` subagents. This mode is always available and requires no special tooling.

1	# Plan Review Rubric (Adversarial)
2	
3	**Used By**: Feasibility, Completeness, and Scope & Alignment reviewers (plan-review-gate)
4	**Purpose**: Binary spec compliance verification for implementation plans against user request and codebase reality
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric is for **adversarial plan review** — a fundamentally different mode from collaborative plan review. Your job is to **find failures** in the plan, not to improve it. You are checking whether the plan meets its contract (the user's request, executed against the real codebase), not whether the plan is "good."
12	
13	**Key distinction:**
14	- **Collaborative review** (`plan-review-rubric.md`): "How can this plan be better?" APPROVED / NEEDS REVISION.
15	- **Adversarial review** (this rubric): "Does this plan meet its contract? Prove it." PASS / FAIL.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Verdict
20	
21	Every adversarial plan review produces exactly one verdict:
22	
23	| Verdict | Meaning | Criteria |
24	| --- | --- | --- |
25	| **PASS** | Plan meets its contract | Zero BLOCKING issues found |
26	| **FAIL** | Plan violates its contract | One or more BLOCKING issues found |
27	
28	There is no "APPROVED WITH COMMENTS." There is no "CHANGES SUGGESTED." PASS or FAIL.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Issue Classification
33	
34	Every issue found is classified as BLOCKING or WARNING:
35	
36	| Classification | Meaning | Impact on Verdict |
37	| --- | --- | --- |
38	| **BLOCKING** | Contract violation — plan claims X, codebase says otherwise, or user asked for X and plan omits it | Causes FAIL |
39	| **WARNING** | Quality concern — not a contract violation but worth noting | Does NOT cause FAIL |
40	
41	**When in doubt, it's BLOCKING.** The threshold for PASS should be high. Err on the side of FAIL.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Evidence Requirements
46	
47	Every finding — whether PASS or FAIL — requires **cited evidence**. Assertions without evidence are invalid.
48	
49	### For PASS (per criterion)
50

1	# Code Review Rubric
2	
3	**Used By**: Code Review Agent
4	**Purpose**: Evaluate code changes before PR creation
5	**Version**: 1.0
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	This rubric ensures code changes meet quality standards before being submitted as a PR. The internal code review catches issues early, before external reviewers (humans, CodeRabbit) see the code.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Severity Levels
16	
17	| Level        | Description                                               | Action                                  |
18	| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
19	| **CRITICAL** | Security vulnerability, data loss risk, breaks production | MUST fix before PR                      |
20	| **HIGH**     | Bug, incorrect behavior, significant performance issue    | MUST fix before PR                      |
21	| **MEDIUM**   | Code smell, maintainability issue, minor bug              | SHOULD fix, discuss if time-constrained |
22	| **LOW**      | Style, nitpick, suggestion                                | OPTIONAL, note for future               |
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Reference Standards
27	
28	| Document                                | Purpose                                  |
29	| --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
30	| `./guides/coding-standards.md`    | Coding standards (source of truth)       |
31	| `CLAUDE.md`                             | Architecture, coding standards, key locs |
32	| `./guides/testing-patterns.md`    | Testing philosophy, mock factories, TDD  |
33	| `test-quality-anti-patterns.md (project-specific)` | Common testing mistakes to avoid         |
34	| TypeScript strict mode                  | Follow conventions from tsconfig.json and ESLint configuration |
35	| `docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`             | Service catalog & factory inventory      |
36	| `templates/task-completion-checklist.md`  | Pre-completion validation steps          |
37	
38	---
39	
40	## Evaluation Categories
41	
42	### 1. Correctness (CRITICAL/HIGH)
43	
44	| Criterion                          | Severity | Check                         |
45	| ---------------------------------- | -------- | ----------------------------- |
46	| Code does what it's supposed to do | CRITICAL | Manual trace through logic    |
47	| Edge cases handled                 | HIGH     | Null, empty, boundary values  |
48	| Error paths correct                | HIGH     | Exceptions caught and handled |
49	| No logic errors                    | CRITICAL | Conditions, loops, recursion  |
50	| State mutations are intentional    | HIGH     | No accidental side effects    |

3	description: Automatic adversarial review gate that spawns 3 independent reviewers in parallel after any plan is drafted - all must PASS before presenting to user
4	auto_activate: true
5	triggers:
6	  - "plan drafted"
7	  - "implementation plan created"

10	---
11	
12	# Plan Review Gate
13	
14	**Core principle**: No plan reaches the user without surviving independent adversarial scrutiny.
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	This skill automatically activates after any implementation plan is drafted, before presenting it to the user. It spawns three independent adversarial reviewers in parallel — Feasibility, Completeness, and Scope & Alignment — each as a fresh `Task()` instance. ALL three must PASS. On failure, the planner incorporates feedback and resubmits to entirely fresh reviewer instances. The gate iterates until consensus or max iterations.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Activation Triggers
23	
24	This skill auto-activates when:
25	
26	1. An implementation plan is drafted (by planner, explorer, or orchestrator)
27	2. The `writing-plans` skill completes a plan
28	3. The orchestrator's plan validation phase produces a plan for review
29	4. User explicitly requests: `/plan-review <path-to-plan>`
30	
31	**Do NOT use for**: Trivial changes (single-file bug fixes, copy edits, config tweaks). The plan must have at least 2 work units or touch 3+ files to warrant gate review.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## The 3 Adversarial Reviewers
36	
37	Each reviewer is a fresh `Task()` instance with read-only access to the codebase. Reviewers produce binary PASS/FAIL verdicts backed by cited evidence. No suggestions — only findings.
38	
39	### Reviewer 1: Feasibility
40	
41	Can this plan actually be executed against the real codebase?
42	
43	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
44	| --- | --- | --- |
45	| File paths exist | BLOCKING if fabricated | Every file path referenced in the plan must exist (verify with glob/grep) |
46	| Dependency ordering correct | BLOCKING if circular or forward-ref | Work units depend only on things that exist or are created before them |
47	| Technical approach matches codebase | BLOCKING if incompatible | Proposed patterns, libraries, and conventions match what the codebase actually uses |
48	| No unstated assumptions | BLOCKING if critical | Plan does not silently depend on services, env vars, or infrastructure that doesn't exist |
49	| File scope realistic | WARNING | Each work unit's file scope is achievable without cascading changes |
50	
51	### Reviewer 2: Completeness
52	
53	Does the plan fully address every aspect of the user's request?
54	
55	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
56	| --- | --- | --- |
57	| All requirements mapped | BLOCKING if gap exists | Every user requirement maps to at least one plan item |
58	| Verification steps defined | BLOCKING if missing | Each change has a way to verify it worked (test, manual check, or assertion) |
59	| Edge cases considered | BLOCKING if obvious gaps | Error scenarios, empty states, boundary conditions addressed |
60	| Rollback/backward compatibility | WARNING | Plan considers what happens if changes need to be reverted |
